summary: I was born in Brooklyn on January 2nd, 1898, right at the turn of the century. I also attended Alexander Hamilton High School there, where I drew cartoons for the annuals and bulletin boards. Brooklyn was an important part of my early life and artistic development.

Winsor McCay was a major influence on me, especially his animation work like 'Gertie the Dinosaur.' I learned that he got his ideas from little photographic animation books, similar to the flipbooks I used as a kid. I even reenacted his performance on a Disney TV show, which was a great honor since his work still exists today.

I worked on a Disney picture where I met Winsor McCay's son, which was a remarkable experience connecting past and present animation. Later, I recreated McCay's 'Gertie the Dinosaur' on a Disney TV show, showing how Disney valued the roots of animation. Disney was the company that eventually succeeded in elevating animation beyond its early novelty days.

The first film I ever saw was at Coney Island, a film of the Nelson-Ganz fight. It was shown in little restaurants where my family and I would eat hot dogs and watch these strange movies. That experience introduced me to early film and animation, including works by the Lumière brothers.

I saw some of the Lumière brothers' films as a child, probably around the time I watched the Nelson-Ganz fight at Coney Island. Their French-produced pictures were among the earliest films I encountered, sometimes even in shocking Dink color. These early cinematic experiences sparked my interest in animation and film.

Wallace Carlson was an animator whose work I saw around 1914 in the Bronx, including 'Dreamy Dud.' He was one of the early pioneers of animated cartoon movies, though not often mentioned today. His films came out about the same time as Winsor McCay's, and they helped shape my early fascination with animation.

'Dreamy Dud' was an animated cartoon by Wallace Carlson that I saw in the Bronx around 1914. It was part of the early animation scene that captivated me as a young viewer. Although these cartoons weren't very funny by today's standards, they were important stepping stones in animation history.

I saw 'Gertie the Dinosaur' at the Cretona Theater in the Bronx, and it made a lasting impression on me. Later, I was able to recreate McCay's performance with 'Gertie' on a Disney TV show, which was a special moment connecting me to animation history. 'Gertie' remains one of the few early animations that still exist today.

entity: Art Students League of New York
summary: I spent about a year at the Art Students League of New York studying under Bridgman, the famous anatomy teacher. Although I studied anatomy and painted a little, I always wanted to be a cartoonist, so I eventually left to pursue animation. The League was an important but brief chapter in my artistic training.

Bridgman was the famous anatomy teacher I studied under at the Art Students League in New York. He was well known for his teaching, though his lessons didn't really rub off on me much. Still, studying anatomy with Bridgman gave me some foundation even though I preferred cartooning.

I lived near Fordham and would often pass a sign on the building opposite the university that said 'Raoul Barre: Cartoonist.' That sign intrigued me so much that I went upstairs and got my first job in animation there around 1916. Fordham was close to where my animation career began.

Raoul Barre was the cartoonist I approached for a job when I was starting out, and he immediately put me to work tracing at his studio. He was a genial Frenchman with a Canadian brogue and ran a small studio of about fifteen people making 'Mutt and Jeff' cartoons. Barre's studio was where I learned the basics of animation and got promoted to animator within weeks.

'Mutt and Jeff' was the cartoon series we produced at Raoul Barre's studio when I started in animation. I began by tracing backgrounds for these cartoons and quickly moved up to animating them. The series was a staple of early animation and gave me valuable experience in the industry.

We sometimes imitated Heinrich Kley's flashy drawing techniques in our animation work at Barre's studio. His style influenced the way we inked drawings on paper before transferring them to celluloid. Kley's work was a source of artistic inspiration for us during those early days.

Milt Gross was one of the famous people who came to work on 'Mutt and Jeff' after I had started there. He was part of a group of talented individuals including Gregory La Cava and magazine men like Nankeville. Gross contributed to the creative development of the cartoons during that period.

Gregory La Cava worked at the Barre studio for a while on 'Mutt and Jeff' cartoons. He was among the notable people who contributed to the studio's output alongside others like Milt Gross. La Cava's involvement added to the creative environment of early animation.
